note: Conversational skill, no installs. It turns a new-project description into a YAML of stack SLOTS (decision categories like 'web framework'), never concrete product picks, and is expected to surface the security/operational slots a naive list misses while omitting slots that don't apply. Run-time interactivity is collapsed: each agent produces a final slots.yaml in one shot, making reasonable best-practice assumptions where the skill would otherwise ask the user.

evals.name: public-api-security-completeness
evals.prompt: I'm about to start a brand-new public REST API in Go (a todo backend that other apps will call). Before I start picking specific libraries, help me map out all the categories of decisions I need to make for the stack. It persists data and is used by third parties. Give me the final list; assume sensible best practices where you'd otherwise ask me.
evals.expected_output: A valid slots.yaml: slots are categories (language/runtime [preference Go], HTTP router/framework, database, authentication, input validation, test framework, logging/observability; optionally rate limiting, containerization). Because it's a public API, authentication AND input-validation slots are present. No slot value is a product name. Required vs optional distinguished.
assertions: Output is a valid slots document (passes validate_yaml.py --stage slots)
Every `slot` value is a decision category, not a product name (e.g. 'web framework', never 'Gin' or 'Postgres')
Includes the security slots a public API needs: at least authentication AND input validation
Records the language/runtime slot with preference Go (the named tool captured as metadata, slot value still a category)
Distinguishes required from optional slots (a `required` boolean that isn't all-true)

evals.name: small-cli-minimalism
evals.prompt: starting a tiny command-line tool in python that reformats messy csv files, just for me, runs locally. what decisions do i actually need to make to set it up? keep it minimal, i don't want a bunch of stuff i don't need. just give me the list.
evals.expected_output: A valid slots.yaml with a MINIMAL set: language/runtime [preference Python], CSV/data handling, CLI/argument parsing, test framework, maybe linter/formatter. Critically does NOT invent web-framework, database, authentication, or deployment slots, which don't apply to a local personal CLI.
assertions: Output is a valid slots document (passes validate_yaml.py --stage slots)
Every `slot` value is a decision category, not a product name
Stays minimal: does NOT include a web-framework, database, or authentication slot (none apply to a local personal CLI)
Records the language/runtime slot with preference Python
Does not pad the list with irrelevant slots to seem thorough

evals.name: webapp-named-tool-and-derived-slots
evals.prompt: I'm building a marketing / SaaS dashboard with Next.js and I want a contact form on it. Help me figure out the full set of stack decisions before I commit to libraries. I know I want Next, the rest is open. Give me the final categorized list.
evals.expected_output: A valid slots.yaml where the web-framework slot is a CATEGORY with preference Next.js (NOT a slot whose value is 'Next.js'). Because of the contact form, it derives form-handling and email-delivery slots (and likely input-validation / spam-protection). Other slots (CSS/styling, testing, hosting) as appropriate.
assertions: Output is a valid slots document (passes validate_yaml.py --stage slots)
The web-framework slot value is a category (e.g. 'web framework') with preference Next.js; it is NOT a slot named 'Next.js'
Derives a form-handling slot AND an email/contact-delivery slot from the stated contact-form requirement
Every other `slot` value is also a category, not a product name
Distinguishes required from optional slots

evals.name: mac-go-cli-small-business
evals.prompt: I'm building a medium-size Go CLI for macOS, for use inside a small business. Map out all the categories of stack decisions I need to make before I pick tools. Give me the final list, assume sensible defaults where you'd otherwise ask.
evals.expected_output: A valid slots.yaml of categories: language/runtime [preference Go], CLI framework, config management, secrets/credential storage, logging, test framework, build/release, macOS code signing & notarization, dependency/supply-chain scanning; optional packaging/distribution, CI. Derives the macOS distribution concern (signing/notarization); includes config/persistence appropriate to a business tool; NO web-framework or GUI slot. No slot value is a product name.
assertions: Output is a valid slots document (passes validate_yaml.py --stage slots)
Every slot value is a decision category, not a product name
Records the language/runtime slot with preference Go
Derives a macOS distribution concern (code signing / notarization, or Homebrew/installer packaging) for shipping a Mac CLI to business users
Scope-appropriate: includes config and/or data-persistence slots (a medium-size business tool, not a throwaway script) but NO web-framework or GUI slot (it is a CLI)

evals.name: crossplatform-desktop-graphs
evals.prompt: I'm building a cross-platform (Windows + macOS) desktop application that consumes structured data and shows high-quality graphs. Help me figure out the full set of stack decisions before I commit to libraries. Final categorized list, sensible defaults where you'd otherwise ask.
evals.expected_output: A valid slots.yaml of categories: language/runtime, desktop UI/GUI framework (app shell), UI component toolkit, charting/data-visualization, structured-data parsing/ingestion, schema validation, state management, test framework, packaging/installer, code signing & notarization (covering both Windows and macOS); optional persistence/cache, HTTP client, auto-update, crash reporting, logging, CI/CD. Derives the charting + data-ingestion slots from the stated requirements and cross-platform packaging/signing for both OSes. No slot value is a product name.
assertions: Output is a valid slots document (passes validate_yaml.py --stage slots)
Every slot value is a decision category, not a product name
Includes a desktop UI / GUI framework (app shell) slot
Includes BOTH a data-visualization/charting slot AND a structured-data ingestion/parsing slot (the two stated requirements)
Derives cross-platform packaging/distribution for BOTH Windows and macOS (installers and/or code signing on each)

evals.name: high-traffic-url-shortener
evals.prompt: I'm building a high-traffic URL shortener. Map out all the categories of stack decisions I need to make before picking specific tools. Final list, assume sensible best practices where you'd otherwise ask me.
evals.expected_output: A valid slots.yaml of categories: language/runtime, HTTP framework/router, short-code generation strategy, primary datastore, caching layer, redirect/URL validation (open-redirect protection), input validation, rate limiting, authentication, logging/observability, deployment platform, test framework, secrets management; optional malicious-URL scanning, analytics datastore, event/streaming pipeline, CDN/edge, custom-domain/TLS, containerization. The 'high traffic' constraint drives caching + a scalable store; derives abuse/security (rate limiting + malicious-link/open-redirect) and click-analytics. No slot value is a product name.
assertions: Output is a valid slots document (passes validate_yaml.py --stage slots)
Every slot value is a decision category, not a product name
The 'high traffic' constraint drives a caching layer slot AND a scalable data store (not just a generic 'database')
Derives abuse/security slots a public shortener needs: at least rate limiting AND malicious-link / open-redirect protection
Includes a click-analytics/tracking slot (core shortener feature) and an observability slot for a high-traffic service
